name: MyLifeBits
short_description: Gordon Bell's decade-long Microsoft Research experiment to digitize and store every document, phone call, image, and biosensor reading of one person's life — the first systematic attempt at a searchable total-recall autobiographical memory.
long_description: Started November 2001 at Microsoft Research, MyLifeBits was Gordon Bell's personal experiment in fulfilling Vannevar Bush's 1945 Memex vision. Bell digitized everything he had ever produced or consumed — articles, books, letters, photos, CDs, home movies, lectures — then extended the system to capture web browsing, phone calls, IM transcripts, television, and radio automatically. By 2006 it also ingested keystrokes, mouse clicks, room audio, and daily imagery from a wearable SenseCam camera. The entire corpus was stored in SQL Server and exposed through full-text search, annotations, clustering, and hyperlinks, letting Bell query his own life the way a search engine queries the web. Jim Gemmell and Roger Lueder built the software infrastructure. The project ran continuously until Bell's death in 2024. As physical AI, MyLifeBits is foundational: it was the first implemented, long-running system to treat personal memory as a queryable database and to combine passive digital capture with wearable sensing, establishing the architecture that later lifelogging products — SenseCam, Narrative Clip, Limitless Pendant — all inherited.
ai_note: The core capture, storage, and search pipeline is database-driven (SQL Server with full-text search), not AI/ML. Later phases incorporated wearable biosensors and the SenseCam image stream, and the project informed AI memory research, but the primary system is a structured personal archive rather than an inference engine.

images.caption: Gordon Bell wearing a white prototype Epiphany Eyewear — a wearable camera system used in the MyLifeBits lifelogging experiment (2013).
images.alt_text: Gordon Bell, a tall older man in glasses, wearing a white wearable camera device around his neck, photographed at a technology event.

sources.title: MyLifeBits — Microsoft Research project page
sources.access_notes: Official project page. Confirmed start date November 2, 2001. Lists Gordon Bell (Emeritus Researcher), Jim Gemmell, and Roger Lueder as key contributors.

sources.title: MyLifeBits: a personal database for everything
sources.publisher: Communications of the ACM, Vol. 49, No. 1, pp. 88–95
sources.access_notes: Authors: Jim Gemmell, Gordon Bell, Roger Lueder. Primary peer-reviewed description of the system. Documents SQL Server backend, SenseCam integration, and full capture scope including keystrokes, mouse clicks, phone calls, IM, and room audio.

sources.title: MyLifeBits — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Secondary overview source. Documents Bell's digitization of all personal records, use of wearable camera, and continuation of the project until Bell's death in 2024.

sources.title: Total Recall: How the E-Memory Revolution Will Change Everything
sources.access_notes: Book by Gordon Bell and Jim Gemmell describing the MyLifeBits experiment, its vision, and implications for memory, work, health, and longevity. Published in paperback as 'Your Life, Uploaded'.

interactions.title: Search your own life like a web page
interactions.description: Bell could type a keyword and retrieve any document, letter, photo, or conversation he had ever encountered — the system indexed decades of personal history in SQL Server with full-text search.

interactions.title: The SenseCam logs everywhere you go
interactions.description: Bell wore a SenseCam — a neck-hung fisheye camera that auto-triggered on changes in light, heat, and motion — which added a continuous visual diary to the archive, capturing hundreds of images a day without any deliberate act.

interactions.title: Annotate a memory with your own voice
interactions.description: Any stored item could receive a spoken or typed annotation directly in the archive, attaching context to a photo, a letter, or a phone call at the moment of retrieval or later reflection.

interactions.title: Clinicians study an aging researcher's biosignals over years
interactions.description: Because the archive spanned health data alongside daily life, later phases of the project informed research into how a long-term personal record could support health monitoring and longitudinal wellness analysis for an aging individual.

interactions.title: Trail from an old paper to everything that cites it
interactions.description: Following Bush's associative trail concept, stored documents could be hyperlinked to anything related — a paper Bell authored in 1970 could be connected to the reviews that arrived by mail, the presentation he gave, and the later work that built on it.

interactions.title: Revisit who you were talking to at any timestamp
interactions.description: Because phone calls and meetings were logged, Bell could reconstruct not just what was said but the social graph of who he communicated with at any point in his career — a queryable contact history spanning decades.

interactions.title: Catch a memory lapse before it becomes a problem
interactions.description: A family member or caregiver could query the archive to see whether an elderly person forgot a medication or appointment, grounded in the system's timestamped capture of daily activity and room audio — the inverse of Bell's own use: someone else reading the record.
